coredata.dc:title: A thermal-assisted electrochemical strategy to synthesize carbon dots with bimodal photoluminescence emission
coredata.dc:creator: Ran, Qingqiang
Wang, Xuepeng
Ling, Peng
Yan, Peiwen
Xu, Jun
Jiang, Long
Wang, Yi
Su, Sheng
Hu, Song
Xiang, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Although several groups have reported bimodal emissive carbon dots (B-CDs) and paid considerable attention to their ratiometric fluorescent sensing application in recent years, the establishment of convenient yet robust strategies that can generate B-CDs from various low-cost and extensive carbon sources is still required. Here a novel thermal-assisted electrochemical strategy for preparing B-CDs from low-cost carbon sources, including graphite and raw coal, is reported. The as-prepared B-CDs exhibit bimodal photoluminescence (PL) emission at 415 and 510 nm under single-wavelength excitation. Furthermore, the origination of the bimodal emissive characteristics of the as-prepared B-CDs was investigated in detail. Under thermal induction, the carbonyl-related surface state PL centers are generated at the surface of B-CDs, and the co-existence of the carbonyl-related surface state and core state PL centers results in bimodal PL emission. Finally, to demonstrate its practical application, a ratiometric PL nanoprobe was developed to sense pH. This thermal-assisted electrochemical strategy is versatile and potentially suitable to a wide range of low-cost carbon sources.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Thermal-assisted
Electrochemical
Bimodal photoluminescence emission
Ratiometric sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Low-magnification TEM image of the B-CDs. The inset is the lateral size distribution of the B-CDs. b) High-magnification TEM image of the B-CDs. c) HRTEM image of the B-CDs. d) AFM image of the B-CDs. The inset is the height distribution of the B-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) FTIR spectrum of the B-CDs. b) XPS spectrum of the B-CDs. c) High-resolution narrow scan spectra of C 1s of the B-CDs. d) High-resolution narrow scan spectra of O 1s of the B-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the B-CDs. b) The pH-dependent PL spectra of the B-CDs (excitation at 300 nm). c) PL spectra of the B-CDs at different excitation wavelengths varied from 280 nm to 380 nm, and d) beyond 400 nm with 20 nm increments under an alkaline environment. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) Fluorescence excitation spectra of the B-CDs (emission wavelengths of 415 nm and 510 nm). b) Fluorescence decay curves of the B-CDs at 415 nm and 510 nm (excitation at 300 nm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) Excitation-dependent PL spectra of the U-CDs when the excitation wavelengths varied from 300 nm to 480 nm. b) A possible mechanism for the thermal-assisted electrochemical cutting of oxidised graphite into B-CDs. c) Excitation-dependent PL spectra of the reduced B-CDs when the excitation wavelengths varied from 300 nm to 480 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) PL spectra of the B-CDs in buffer solution at different pH values (10.6–13.4) under the excitation of 300 nm. b) Linear plots of I415 nm/I510 nm of the B-CDs as a function of pH values. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic diagram of the thermal-assisted electrochemical strategy to synthesise the B-CDs from graphite.

head.ce:title: A thermal-assisted electrochemical strategy to synthesize carbon dots with bimodal photoluminescence emission

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Coal Combustion, Department of Energy and Power Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Coal Combustion
Department of Energy and Power Engineering
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Coal Combustion, Department of Energy and Power Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, 430074, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, University of California, Los Angeles, CA, 90095, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, University of California, Los Angeles, CA, 90095, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although several groups have reported bimodal emissive carbon dots (B-CDs) and paid considerable attention to their ratiometric fluorescent sensing application in recent years, the establishment of convenient yet robust strategies that can generate B-CDs from various low-cost and extensive carbon sources is still required. Here a novel thermal-assisted electrochemical strategy for preparing B-CDs from low-cost carbon sources, including graphite and raw coal, is reported. The as-prepared B-CDs exhibit bimodal photoluminescence (PL) emission at 415 and 510 nm under single-wavelength excitation. Furthermore, the origination of the bimodal emissive characteristics of the as-prepared B-CDs was investigated in detail. Under thermal induction, the carbonyl-related surface state PL centers are generated at the surface of B-CDs, and the co-existence of the carbonyl-related surface state and core state PL centers results in bimodal PL emission. Finally, to demonstrate its practical application, a ratiometric PL nanoprobe was developed to sense pH. This thermal-assisted electrochemical strategy is versatile and potentially suitable to a wide range of low-cost carbon sources.

A thermal-assisted electrochemical oxidation strategy is developed to produce bimodal photoluminescence emissive carbon dots from the blocky graphite. Under thermal induction, the carbonyl pairs are generated at the surface of carbon dots, and the co-existence of the carbonyl-related surface state and core state PL centers results in bimodal PL emission. This approach is versatile, and potentially suitable to a wide range of low-cost carbon sources.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), also named carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are a novel kind of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with sizes less than 10 nm in diameter [1–4]. Because of their attractive merits, including tunable photoluminescence (PL) emission properties, excellent water solubility, good electrical conductivity, extensive surface area, and convenient surface modification, CDs have attracted considerable attention over the past few years in the fields of electronic, catalysis, information encryption, photovoltaic devices, batteries, supercapacitors and so on [5–11]. In particular, compared with their traditional semiconducting quantum dots counterparts, such as CdSe, and CdS quantum dots, CDs exhibit excellent photostability against photobleaching, no blinking, low toxicity, and admirable biocompatibility, which situate them to be promising fluorescent nanomaterials for analytical sensing applications, especially in vivo or intracellular sensing [12–15]. Generally, CDs are composed of two parts: internal carbon core and surface functional groups. When certain surface functional groups of CDs bind to the specific analytes, the interaction between CDs and analytes can change the PL intensity of CDs. Thus, the detection of the analytes can be realised by measuring the change of PL intensity of the CDs. Nowadays, numerous fluorescent nanoprobes based on CDs have been successfully constructed and applied to detect anions, cations, and various biomolecules in solution or/and biological samples [16–20]. For example, Liu et al. synthesised CDs by microwave irradiation of glucose and poly(ethylene glycol)-200, and they found that the PL intensity of the as-prepared CDs linearly changed with the content of F− in the range of 0.1–10 μM [16]. Gao et al. [17] synthesised CDs with red PL emission by the one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and neutral red. The as-prepared CDs were applied to detect Pt2+, Au3+, and Pd2+, and correspondingly the detection limits can be 0.886, 3.03, and 3.29 μM, respectively [17]. Furthermore, the CDs were also successfully used in the bioimaging and biosensing of Pt2+ in PC12 cells and zebrafish. Li and co-workers reported a turn off-on fluorescent nanoprobe consisting of CDs functionalised with (2,4-dinitrophenoxy)tyrosine for sensitive and selective intracellular sensing of hydrogen sulfide (H2S) [18]. A detection limit as low as 0.05 nM was achieved, highlighting the significance of this fluorescent nanoprobe because the H2S level is low in our body. However, most of the reported CDs only displayed one broad PL emission peak, which rendered these CD-based fluorescent nanoprobes usually dependent on the change of the unimodal PL emission intensity. The sensing accuracy for analytes may be negatively interfered with by various analyte-independent factors such as instrumental parameters, the microenvironmental around the probes, the local concentration of the probes, and the background signal [21–24]. For instance, Li et al. found that the quenching effect of PL of the CDs by mental ions can be strengthened by increasing environmental pH [21]. They attributed this phenomenon to the evolution of functional groups of CDs to hydroxyl with increased environmental pH. Hydroxyl can contribute to the quenching by reacting with the quencher, such as Fe3+. Yu et al. conducted temperature-dependent PL measurements of CDs from cryogenic to room temperatures, and found that the PL intensity decreased upon increasing temperature [22]. Chen et al. reported a concentration-dependent colour-tunable PL phenomenon in CDs and found that the PL emission wavelength of the CDs with different concentrations can be tuned from 585 to 514 nm under the fixed excitation of 420 nm [23]. Thus, to provide precise and quantitative analyses, the establishment of convenient yet robust ways for constructing advanced CD-based fluorescent nanoprobes with high sensitivity to analytes and inherent reliability against analyte-independent factors are fundamentally significant.

One practical approach to overcome the interferences from the analyte-independent factors is to take advantage of the ratiometric strategy. Ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes utilise the relationship between the intensity ratio of two well-resolved PL emission peaks and the analytes, which can provide built-in correction for the interferences, significantly increasing the sensitivity and improving the quantification accuracy. Thus, they have significant advantages over the fluorescent probes that are dependent on the change of the unimodal PL emission intensity [25–27]. To date, the commonly adopted CD-based ratiometric fluorescent probes are usually constructed by coupling unimodal PL emissive CDs (U-CDs) with other emitters that have different emission wavelengths together. In the context of CD-based ratiometric fluorescent sensing, various samples, including fluorescent small organic molecules, fluorescent proteins, semiconducting quantum dots, metal clusters, and metal-organic framework, have been tried [28–32]. Unfortunately, these methods often suffer from complex and tedious synthesis routes and inevitably brought in the drawbacks of organic dyes or semiconductor quantum dots with heavy metal cores and so on, which weakened the merits of CDs. Another efficient way to construct the ratiometric sensors is using the intrinsic bimodal PL emissive CDs (B-CDs), which simultaneously perform two fluorescent peaks without integrating with other emitters. Thus the B-CDs can be considered natural ratiometric sensors. Unfortunately, the synthetization of B-CDs is not easy, and few studies successfully prepared of the B-CDs for ratiometric sensing [33–40]. For instance, Ma et al. [33] synthesised B-CDs through hydrothermal treatment of m-aminophenol and oxalic acid. The as-prepared B-CDs exhibited two PL emission peaks at 430 and 510 nm under the single excitation wavelength of 380 nm [33]. Furthermore, the as-prepared B-CDs were applied to the ratiometric fluorescent determination of Cr6+ in textile, steel, industrial wastewater, and chromium residue samples. Shangguan et al. developed label-free B-CDs as ratiometric fluorescent pH nanoprobes by one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and basic fuchsin and then applied them for intracellular pH sensing [34]. Song et al. [35] prepared B-CDs through one-pot hydrothermal carbonisation of o-phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid. These B-CDs can respond to pH and lysine by distinctive emission peaks, and thus they can be applied for ratiometric detection of two targets under specific PL emission wavelengths [35]. Li et al. fabricated B-CDs via the hydrothermal treatment of alizarin carmine and demonstrated their applications for ratiometric glutathione (GSH) sensing and differentiating cancer cells from normal cells [36]. From these above studies, it can be drawn that the intrinsic B-CD synthesis is mainly based on hydrothermal treatment of small molecular precursors involving high temperatures, high pressures, long reaction times, or toxic organic solvents. The high temperature and pressure during the hydrothermal preparation of B-CDs may result in some reaction pathways that are not predictable, forming by-products that need for more tedious purification and separation process. Besides, the choice of precursors used to prepare B-CDs is still based on a trial-and-error strategy, which leads to the limitations for precursor selection, long and difficult optimisations of the desired properties, extensive time consumption, and high economic cost. To go beyond these limitations, green strategies that can employ a wide range of low-cost and extensive precursors to generate CDs with the bimodal PL emission characteristics with high reproducibility are required.

In this work, a novel and facile thermal-assisted electrochemical strategy was developed to synthesise B-CDs from graphite by electrochemical oxidation under a mild thermal-assisted environment (80 °C) for the first time (Scheme 1 ). The as-prepared B-CDs displayed two distinct PL emissions located at 415 nm and 510 nm under the single excitation wavelength of 300 nm and excellent stability under different environmental conditions. Furthermore, the origination of the bimodal emissive characteristics of the as-prepared B-CDs was investigated in detail. It shows that under thermal induction, the carbonyl-related surface state PL centers are generated at the surface of CDs, and the co-existence of the carbonyl-related surface state and core state PL centers results in the bimodal PL emission. To demonstrate its practical application, we developed a ratiometric PL sensor for the sensing of pH under an alkaline environment. Besides, instead of graphite, raw coal, a cheaper and more abundant material, was applied as the carbon source, and B-CDs with similar characteristics can also be acquired, further demonstrating the adaptability of this thermal-assisted electrochemical strategy. This study introduces a novel thermal-assisted electrochemical strategy, providing new ideas for preparing the B-CDs from a wide range of low-cost carbon sources without the requirement of tedious synthetic processes or the use of toxic/expensive solvents.

Unless otherwise noted, all chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Potassium hydroxide (KOH), potassium chloride (KCl), potassium bromide (KBr), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), carboxymethylcellulose sodium (CMC-Na) and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (US). Graphite working electrodes (99.99%), Pt sheet counter electrodes (10∗10∗0.3 mm), Hg/HgO reference electrodes, Pt electrode holders and double-layer electrochemical cells (50 mL) were purchased from Wuhan GaussUnion Co. Ltd (Wuhan, China). Ultrapure water of 18.25 MΩ cm was used for all experiments.

The thermal-assisted electrochemical preparation of B-CDs was performed in a double-layer electrochemical cell with 2 M KOH as the supporting electrolyte. The circulating liquid at 80 °C continuously flowing between the layers of the double-layer electrochemical cell. Graphite rod, Pt sheet electrode, and Hg/HgO electrode were used as working electrode, counter electrode, and reference electrode, respectively. The applied potential was cycled 120 times between 0 and 2.0 V at a scan rate of 0.06 V/s. After completion of the thermal-assisted electrochemical oxidation process, the soluble product was purified by vacuum filtration and then dialysed by a cellulose ester membrane bag (1000 Da) for 5 days to remove the ions completely. Finally, the collected B-CDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further utilisation. The yield of B-CDs was calculated by dividing the mass of as-prepared B-CDs by the mass difference of graphite rod before and after the reaction, and it was approximately 52.5%. In the control experiments, room temperature (25 °C) circulating liquid flowing between the two electrochemical cell layers was adopted to prepare U-CDs. Other procedures were the same as that of B-CDs. Moreover, to elucidate the effect of the surface structure of the B-CDs on their bimodal PL emission behaviour, NaBH4 was added in excess to the B-CDs aqueous solution to obtain reduced B-CDs. After stirring at room temperature for 24 h, the product was dialysed with ultra-pure water using a cellulose ester membrane bag (1000 Da) to remove the ions completely. Furthermore, to demonstrate the adaptability of this thermal-assisted electrochemical strategy, a coal slice was used to replace the graphite rod to prepare the B-CDs. In this experiment, 90 mg Shanxi coal (a typical Chinese coal) powder (200 mesh) and 10 mg CMC-Na were firstly mixed intensively. Then, the powder mixture was pressed into a sheet at 15 MPa. A Pt electrode holder was used to clamp the coal sheet as the working electrode, and other procedures were the same as that of graphite-derived B-CDs.

TEM and HRTEM images of the CDs were obtained on a Talos F200X field-emission transmission electron microscope at 200 kV (FEI Company, Netherlands). AFM images of the CDs were obtained on a SPM 9700 (Shimadzu, Japan). FTIR spectra of CDs were tested in a Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, US) by mixing the CDs solution with KBr. XPS spectra were obtained on an EscaLab Xi + spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, US). The UV–vis absorption spectra were surveyed on a Lambda 35 UV–vis spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, US). The PL spectra and PLE spectra were recorded by a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer (Varian, US), and the slit width for excitation and emission were set at 10 nm. The fluorescence lifetimes were measured by an FLS 980 series steady/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The absolute PL quantum yield (QY) of B-CDs was measured using a Hamamatsu C9920-02G absolute PL quantum yield measurement system (Hamamatsu Photonics, Japan) equipped with a calibrated integrating sphere.

For the fluorescence assay of pH values, the as-prepared B-CDs were added into 3.0 mL of a buffer solution with various pH values and the mixture was shaken thoroughly. The PL spectra were recorded after 2 min. Without a special statement, the final concentration of B-CDs is 0.1 mg/mL. All the PL spectra were recorded under the excitation at 300 nm and performed at room temperature.

The morphology, lateral particle size, crystal structure, and particle height of the B-CDs were characterised by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), and atomic force microscope (AFM). The TEM images show that the B-CDs with a quasi-spherical shape are uniform and well dispersed (Fig. 1 a and b). As demonstrated by the inset in Fig. 1a, the majority of the B-CDs have a narrow lateral size distribution in the range from 1.1 to 2.3 nm. Based on the statistical analysis of one hundred particles, the average lateral size of the B-CDs is 1.6 ± 0.3 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1c) clearly shows lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.343 nm, which is consistent with that of the (002) facet of graphite. Moreover, as the AFM image (Fig. 1d) revealed, the average height of the B-CDs is about 1.6 nm, which is comparable to the lateral size of the B-CDs, demonstrating that the B-CDs are quasi-spherical shapes.

The surface functional groups and element states of B-CDs were characterised by FTIR and XPS. As depicted in the FTIR spectrum of B-CDs (Fig. 2 a), the broad absorption bands at 3200-3400 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups(−OH). The peaks at 2850 and 2920 cm−1 originate from the stretching vibrations of C–H bonds. The characteristic absorption peaks at 1745, 1590, 1427, and 905 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CC bonds, and the in-plane and out-of-plane bending vibrations of –OH on aromatic carboxyl groups (−COOH), respectively. The existence of two peaks at 1250 and 1120 cm−1 indicate the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of C–O bonds, respectively. Benefitting from a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface, B-CDs could be easily dispersed in an aqueous solution and applicable for sensing and bio-imaging. The XPS analysis can further support these FTIR results. As shown in Fig. 2b, the B-CDs mainly contain carbon and oxygen elements with corresponding contents of 70.87% and 29.13%, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s of B-CDs can be fitted by four peaks at 284.8, 286.2, 287.9, and 289.1 eV (Fig. 2c), contributing to CC, C–O, CO bonds, and –COOH group, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s of B-CDs can be fitted into two peaks at 531.9 and 533.8 eV corresponding to CO and C–O bonds, respectively (Fig. 2d).

The optical properties of the B-CD solution are shown in Fig. 3 . As shown in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3a), the distinct absorption peak around 230 nm can be attributed to the π-π∗ transition of the sp2 carbon domains, which is considered an intrinsic core-state. Also, the wide and weak absorption band in the range of 280–400 nm can be assigned to the n-π∗ transition of the carbonyl relevant moieties on the surface of B-CDs, which is considered a surface-state [41]. To further explore the luminescence properties of B-CDs, the pH-dependent PL spectra were recorded. Interestingly, the unique bimodal PL emission behaviour of B-CDs could be seen when pH increases from 2 to 12 (Fig. 3b). Under excitation wavelength at 300 nm, the PL emission peaks of B-CDs are composed of two partially overlapping peaks at 415 nm and 510 nm, which can be attributed to the core-state and the surface-state PL emission, respectively. It should be noted that the B-CDs display a more easily distinguishable bimodal profile under alkaline conditions compared to acid conditions. This difference may be caused by the effect of environmental pH on the protonation or deprotonation process of surface functional groups of B-CDs [42]. The PL spectra of the B-CDs exhibiting two peaks at 415 nm and 510 nm at the excitation wavelengths varied from 280 to 380 nm with 20 nm increments are shown in Fig. 3c. Unlike most previously reported CDs, the B-CDs display an excitation-independent feature, and the dual emission peaks are not shifted under different excitation wavelengths. Furthermore, when the excitation wavelength is beyond 400 nm (Fig. 3d), the B-CDs only process one PL emission peak around 510 nm, which is mainly because the excitation energy is not sufficient to drive the π-π∗ transition. It should be noted that the PL intensity of B-CDs increases with the increase of the excitation wavelength from 400 nm to 440 nm and then decreases with further increasing the excitation wavelength from 440 nm to 480 nm, exhibiting a maximum excitation wavelength near 440 nm. In addition, the absolute PL quantum yield (QY) of the B-CDs in aqueous solution at room temperature is about 3.0%, which is comparable to many CDs produced by the electrochemical method reported in the literature [43,44].

Further, the PL excitation (PLE) spectra of B-CDs in solution were tested. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the excitation peak of emission at 415 nm is centred at 325 nm, while the emission at 510 nm has two excitation peaks located at 330 and 430 nm. As shown in Fig. 4b, time-resolved PL decay curves were measured for the 415 nm and 510 nm PL emissions of the B-CDs by 300 nm excitation. Generally, fluorescence lifetime is an intrinsic property of CDs depending on the nature of the PL sites and the microenvironment. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs can be calculated from measured PL decay curves. The PL decay curves can be fitted to two exponential functions using the following equation [6]:
(1)
I(t) = B
1 exp(−t/τ
1 ) + B
2 exp(−t/τ
2 )
where τ
1 and τ
2 refer to fluorescence decay lifetimes, and B
1 and B
2 are the pre-exponential factors of τ
1 and τ
2 , respectively. The average fluorescence lifetimes at 415 nm and 510 nm calculated are 2.38 ns and 4.06 ns, respectively. These results indicate that the two well-resolved PL emission peaks under 300 nm excitation are originated from different electronic transition processes and might generate different PL signal responses to the external physical or chemical stimulant. The as-prepared B-CDs are promising for using as the ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes based on this bimodal PL emission property [45].

To further determine the mechanism of the bimodal PL emission properties, we prepared U-CDs under room temperature as a comparative trial. As shown in Fig. 5 a, U-CDs display excitation-dependent unimodal PL emission, which is similar to most of the CDs that have been reported. In addition, the unimodal PL emission characteristics remain constant even under alkaline conditions (Fig. S1). TEM, HRTEM images, and FTIR spectra of the U-CDs were also measured and compared with those of the B-CDs. The quasi-spherical morphology (Fig. S2) and layer spacing (Fig. S3) about 0.32 nm of the U-CDs are similar to those of the B-CDs. However, U-CDs have a larger size distribution compared with B-CDs. This may be the reason why the PL emission peak of the U-CDs is red-shifted compared with the core-state PL emission peak of the B-CDs (Fig. S4).

Furthermore, there are significant differences in surface chemical structures between U-CDs and B-CDs. As FTIR spectra displayed, the reduced absorption at 3400 and 1745 cm−1 (−OH stretching vibration from carboxyl groups and CO stretching vibrations) and increased absorption at 1120 and 980 cm−1 (C–O stretching vibrations and epoxy groups) in FTIR spectra from the U-CDs to B-CDs imply the loss of epoxy groups and production of more carboxyl groups in the B-CDs (Fig. S5). According to the above discussions, we speculate the possible mechanism for the thermal-assisted electrochemical cutting of blocky graphite into B-CDs. At first, when oxygen atoms bind to a graphite surface under the oxidation condition, they form two-legged epoxy bridges, which line up to lower their energy. This exerts a collective tension, breaking the underlying carbon-carbon bonds [46,47]. Then, the ring-opening addition reaction between the epoxy group and the hydroxyl group in the basic electrolyte can be happened with mild thermal assistance, forming a hydroxyl pair that is connected to the edge carbon atoms. Next, the hydroxyl pair further convert to carbonyl pair by electrochemical oxidisation process. Finally, the B-CDs are consequently produced with the flakes separated from the blocky graphite and contain a relatively large sp2 (graphene-like) structure core and with carbonyl groups anchored on their surface/edge (Fig. 5b).

To further verify the above mechanism of the B-CDs prepared by the thermal-assisted electrochemical strategy, the B-CDs were reduced with excessive NaBH4. This reduction reaction will not result in a significant change in the size of B-CDs, but can selectively reduce the carbonyl and epoxy moieties to hydroxyl groups [48]. As shown in Fig. 5c, the PL spectra of the reduced B-CDs are very similar to those of the U-CDs. Both bimodal PL emission and strong long-wavelength PL emission properties are disappeared. These results strongly demonstrate that the PL emission peak around 510 nm originates from the carbonyl-relevant moieties on the surface of B-CDs. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S6, the PL spectra of coal-derived B-CDs are similar to those of the graphite-derived B-CDs, indicating that this mechanism also applies to other carbon sources with graphitic structure, and this thermal-assisted electrochemical strategy is versatile and potentially suitable to a wide range of low-cost carbon sources.

Additionally, the stability of the B-CDs was also investigated. When the light illumination time is 1 h under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. S7), the PL intensities of the peak at 415 nm (peak415nm) and peak at 510 nm (peak510nm) are almost unchanged, indicating that the B-CDs exhibited good photostability against photobleaching. Moreover, the B-CDs show excellent stability against the high ionic concentration, and its PL intensity does not change even in a 1 M KCl medium (Fig. S8). It should be noted that the PL intensities of the peak415nm and peak510nm gradually decrease with the increase of the environmental temperature from 25 to 60 °C. These temperature-dependent behaviours may be caused by the increase of the nonradiative recombination under high temperatures (Fig. S9). However, the ratio of the intensities of peak415nm and peak510nm remains unchanged, proving that the B-CDs as the ratiometric fluorescent probes can have good resistance to environmental interference. Besides, no aggregate precipitate is produced after storing under 4 °C for two months, and the PL properties of the B-CDs are almost unchanged (Fig. S10). In summary, the B-CDs have significantly excellent stability, which provides the possibility to be applied in complex environments.

The pH sensing capacity of the B-CDs was further studied under 300 nm excitation. From Fig. 6 a, it can be seen that with increasing pH values from 10.6 to 13.4, the intensity of peak415nm changes moderately while that of peak510nm changes significantly, indicating that increasing pH value has little effect on B-CDs core but a significant effect on B-CDs surface due to the deprotonation of carboxyl groups. The deprotonation leads to the formation of a delocalized π bond and the increase of n electrons. As shown in Fig. 6b, the intensity ratio of the dual fluorescence peaks (I415 nm/I510 nm) increases linearly and rapidly for the pH values from 10.6 to 13.4, illustrating that the B-CDs have strong pH-dependent behaviour and are suitable for sensing pH in alkaline environments. Distinctly, there is a good linear relationship between I415 nm/I510 nm and pH in the range of 10.6–13.4, according to the linear equation: I415 nm/I510 nm = −0.94382 + 0.16334 pH, R2 = 0.988. The results indicate that B-CDs could be used as a ratiometric fluorescent pH nanoprobe within the range of 10.6–13.4. It needs to point out that some CD-based ratiometric pH sensors that respond over the mild pH range of 3–11 have been reported [49–53]. Even more, other reliable pH sensing methods have been reported in recent years, such as fluorescence lifetime pH sensing [54]. However, very few sensors can be applied under extreme alkaline conditions, which is probably because of the inability of the materials to maintain photochemical stability under extreme alkaline conditions. There are significant needs for rapid and accurate measurements at extreme alkaline environments, examples of which include the monitoring of Soda Lake, hydrothermal vents, hindgut of insects, alkalophilic microorganisms, and alkaline wastewater produced by industrial activity, such as electroplating process, cement production, processing of bauxite, and preparation of indigo dye [55–58]. Thus, although the detecting range of pH is not as comprehensive as some researches, the as-prepared B-CDs are expected to be used as a specialised ratiometric pH sensor under strong alkaline conditions.

In summary, a novel and universal thermal-assisted electrochemical strategy for preparing B-CDs from low-cost carbon sources, including graphite and raw coal, is reported for the first time. The as-prepared B-CDs demonstrate unique bimodal PL emission behaviours at 415 and 510 nm at the excitation wavelengths from 280 to 380 nm and have excellent stability under different environmental conditions. The emerging PL emission peak at 510 nm of the B-CDs should be originated from the carbonyl relevant moieties on the surface of the B-CDs. Under thermal-assisted electrochemical conditions, the carbonyl-related surface state PL centers are generated at the surface of B-CDs. The co-existence of the carbonyl-related surface state and core state PL centers results in the bimodal PL emission. Moreover, the B-CDs have been developed as a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe for precise and quantitative sensing the pH values in a strong alkaline environment by monitoring the intensity ratio of the dual fluorescence peaks (I415 nm/I510 nm) under 300 nm excitation. This research shows that the introduction of thermal assist enables the CDs to generate carbonyl-related surface state PL centers and display bimodal PL emission, and this method has extensive prospects for large-scale production of B-CDs.

Qingqiang Ran: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Xuepeng Wang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Resources. Peng Ling: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Peiwen Yan: Writing – review & editing. Jun Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources. Long Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yi Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Sheng Su: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Song Hu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Jun Xiang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2019YFB1503902) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (No.52176110). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for the measurements.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2022.03.041.
